dashboard.label: Configuration Guide
dashboard.description: Platform-specific instructions for getting AI/LLM data into Splunk

div.p: The ATLAS detection rules require AI/LLM telemetry in your Splunk indexes. This guide covers how to configure each major platform to send the right data.
Tier 1 rules (volumetric and operational) work with standard telemetry — token counts, API call metadata, and storage access logs. Most platforms emit this by default.
Tier 2 rules (content inspection) require the actual prompt text in the log events. This requires explicit opt-in on every platform listed below.
LiteLLM is the most widely adopted open-source LLM proxy. It standardizes output to the OpenAI format across 100+ providers.
LiteLLM logs to PostgreSQL by default. To get data into Splunk, use one of these approaches:

ul.li: Custom callback → HEC: Write a LiteLLM success_callback that POSTs JSON events to a Splunk HTTP Event Collector (HEC) endpoint. This is the most direct path.
JSON file logging → Universal Forwarder: Enable json_logs: true in your LiteLLM config. Monitor the log file with a Splunk Universal Forwarder.
DB Connect: Use Splunk DB Connect to query the LiteLLM_SpendLogs table on a schedule.
OpenTelemetry: LiteLLM supports OpenTelemetry. Export to the Splunk OTel Collector. Note: Splunk Observability Cloud has native LiteLLM support.

p.strong: Enable Prompt Logging (required for Tier 2 rules):

div.p: Add to your proxy_config.yaml:
Without this setting, messages and response fields are empty in LiteLLM logs.
Key Fields: model, user, api_key, spend, total_tokens, prompt_tokens, completion_tokens, call_type, startTime, endTime, messages (opt-in), response (opt-in)
Recommended sourcetype: litellm_proxy or llm_gateway
Azure OpenAI emits three log categories via Azure Diagnostic Settings: audit logs, request/response logs, and trace logs.

ul.li: Enable Diagnostic Settings on your Azure OpenAI resource
Stream to Azure Event Hub
Use the Splunk Azure Monitor Logs Function or Splunk Cloud Data Manager to route Event Hub events to Splunk HEC

div.p: Important limitation: Azure OpenAI diagnostic logs do not include prompt or response text. Fields available: OperationName, CallerIPAddress (partially masked), statusCode, DurationMs. This data supports Tier 1 rules only.

For Tier 2 rules: Place Azure API Management (APIM) in front of your Azure OpenAI instance. Configure APIM request/response logging to capture the full prompt and response body, then route APIM logs to Splunk.
Sourcetype (via APIM): Configure as azure_openai or match your naming convention
AWS Bedrock provides CloudWatch metrics by default. For detailed logging, enable model invocation logging.

ul.li: CloudWatch Metrics (Tier 1): Use the Splunk Add-on for AWS to pull CloudWatch metrics (invocation count, latency, error rates, token usage)

Model Invocation Logging (Tier 2): Enable in Bedrock console → Settings → Model invocation logging. Select S3 or CloudWatch as destination. Route to Splunk via Splunk Add-on for AWS (S3 input or CloudWatch Logs input)

p.strong: Enable Prompt Logging (required for Tier 2 rules):

div.p: In the Bedrock console, enable model invocation logging with "Log request body" and "Log response body" checked. Logs are written to S3 or CloudWatch in JSON format.
Key Fields: modelId, inputTokenCount, outputTokenCount, inputBodyJson (opt-in), outputBodyJson (opt-in), status, region
Recommended sourcetype: bedrock_logs or aws:cloudwatch:logs
The OpenAI API does not emit server-side logs to customers. All logging must happen at the application layer.

ul.li: Application-level logging: Instrument your code to log each API call (request, response, tokens, user, timestamp) and send to Splunk via HEC or a local log file monitored by Universal Forwarder
OpenTelemetry: Use the OpenTelemetry OpenAI instrumentation (opentelemetry-instrumentation-openai Python package) to auto-capture API calls as spans. Export to Splunk OTel Collector
Use a gateway instead: Place LiteLLM, Portkey, or Helicone in front of your OpenAI calls — they handle logging centrally

div.p: For Tier 2 rules: Your application logging must include the actual prompt text. OpenTelemetry instrumentation can capture this if message content capture is enabled.
Vertex AI provides Cloud Audit Logs and Cloud Monitoring metrics by default.

ul.li: Enable Data Access Audit Logs for the Vertex AI API in the GCP IAM console
Create a Pub/Sub topic and a log sink that routes Vertex AI logs to Pub/Sub
Use the Splunk Add-on for Google Cloud Platform to ingest from Pub/Sub

div.p: Important limitation: Vertex AI audit logs record API call metadata (caller identity, method, resource name, status) but do not include prompt or response content by default. For Tier 2 rules, implement application-level logging or use an LLM gateway in front of Vertex AI.
Key Fields: protoPayload.methodName, protoPayload.authenticationInfo.principalEmail, protoPayload.status, resource.labels.model_id
Kong AI Gateway extends Kong's API management platform for LLM traffic, with built-in logging plugins.

ul.li: Enable the Kong http-log plugin pointed at a Splunk HEC endpoint
Alternatively, use the file-log plugin and monitor with a Universal Forwarder
Kong also supports OpenTelemetry export via the opentelemetry plugin

div.p: For Tier 2 rules: The http-log plugin can be configured to include request and response bodies. Enable the ai-request-transformer and ai-response-transformer plugins to capture prompt content.
Recommended sourcetype: kong:ai or llm_gateway
Portkey is an AI gateway with built-in observability, guardrails, and detailed request logging.

ul.li: Use Portkey's webhook integration to forward request logs to a Splunk HEC endpoint
Alternatively, use Portkey's API (/logs endpoint) to pull logs on a schedule via a scripted input

div.p: For Tier 2 rules: Portkey logs request and response content by default in its platform. Ensure your webhook or API export includes the request.messages and response.choices fields.
Key Fields: model, provider, request.messages, response.choices, tokens.prompt, tokens.completion, cost, latency, status
Recommended sourcetype: portkey or llm_gateway
Helicone is a drop-in proxy for OpenAI-compatible APIs with built-in monitoring and observability.

ul.li: Use Helicone's webhook feature to forward request events to a Splunk HEC endpoint
Alternatively, use the Helicone API to export request logs to Splunk via scripted input

div.p: For Tier 2 rules: Helicone captures full request and response content by default. Ensure your export includes prompt and completion text fields.
Key Fields: model, user_id, request_body, response_body, prompt_tokens, completion_tokens, total_tokens, latency, status
Recommended sourcetype: helicone or llm_gateway
Cloudflare AI Gateway provides caching, rate limiting, and analytics for LLM API calls.

ul.li: Use Cloudflare Logpush to forward AI Gateway logs to a Splunk HEC endpoint or S3 bucket monitored by Splunk
Alternatively, use the Cloudflare API to pull gateway analytics on a schedule

div.p: For Tier 2 rules: Cloudflare AI Gateway supports logging request/response content. Enable logging in your gateway configuration and ensure Logpush includes the content fields.
Recommended sourcetype: cloudflare:ai_gateway or llm_gateway
Like OpenAI, the Anthropic API does not emit server-side logs to customers. Logging must happen at the application layer or via a gateway.

ul.li: Instrument your application code to log each API request/response to Splunk via HEC
Use OpenTelemetry with the Anthropic instrumentation package
Place LiteLLM or another gateway in front for centralized logging

div.p: Recommended sourcetype: anthropic_logs or llm_gateway
Self-hosted inference servers typically log to stdout/stderr or local files.

ul.li: Monitor the inference server's log output with a Splunk Universal Forwarder
For structured logging, place LiteLLM in front as a unified gateway — it supports Ollama, vLLM, and TGI as backends
For Kubernetes deployments, use the Splunk OTel Collector to capture container logs

div.p: For Tier 2 rules: Ollama and vLLM log request content at debug log level. Enable debug logging and ensure the forwarder captures the full log entry. For production use, a gateway is recommended over debug-level logging.
Recommended sourcetype: ollama, vllm, or llm_gateway
div.h2: Custom API Gateway or Reverse Proxy
div.p: Many organizations route LLM traffic through an existing API gateway (NGINX, Envoy, AWS API Gateway, Azure APIM, FastAPI) where they can add logging.
div.pre: _time, src_ip, user, model, total_tokens, prompt_tokens, completion_tokens, status, session_id, event_type
user_input OR prompt OR request_text OR message response OR completion safety_flagged, finish_reason
div.p: Log events as JSON and send to Splunk via HEC with KV_MODE=json for automatic field extraction.
Questions or issues with data configuration: contact@gicengineeringconsultants.com
